l1_1.hook: You inherit $50,000 today. A financial advisor offers you a guaranteed $70,000 in five years instead. Without any other information, is that deal good, fair, or terrible? You cannot answer that question without understanding the time value of money.

terms.d: The current worth of a future sum of money, discounted at a required rate of return.
The value of an asset at a specified date in the future, assuming a rate of growth.
The rate used to convert future cash flows into their present-day equivalent.
Earning returns on previously earned returns, creating exponential rather than linear growth.
The value of the best foregone alternative when a choice is made.
A series of equal cash flows at regular intervals, valued using TVM principles.

sections.h: Why a Dollar Today Is Worth More Than a Dollar Tomorrow
sections.body: The Time Value of Money (TVM) is the foundational axiom of all finance: a unit of currency available today is worth more than the same unit available in the future. This is not inflation — it is the concept of opportunity cost. A dollar in hand today can be invested to produce additional dollars. This principle underlies every significant financial decision: mortgage pricing, corporate capital budgeting, pension fund liability calculations, and the valuation of every asset class on earth.

TVM mathematics rest on two equations. Future Value asks: given an amount today, what will it grow to? Present Value asks the inverse: given an amount in the future, what is it worth in today's terms?
sections.formula:
FV  =  PV × (1 + r)ⁿ
PV  =  FV ÷ (1 + r)ⁿ

Where: r = periodic interest rate | n = number of periods

callout.label: Key Insight
callout.text: The discount rate r is the critical variable. It encodes your opportunity cost — the return you could earn on an alternative of equivalent risk. A higher r means you require more compensation for waiting, which mathematically reduces the present value of any future cash flow.

sections.body: Interest can compound annually, semi-annually, monthly, daily, or continuously. The effective annual rate (EAR) captures the impact of compounding frequency:
sections.formula:
EAR  =  (1 + r/m)^m  −  1

Where: m = compounding periods per year
Continuous compounding:  FV = PV × e^(rt)

example.label: Worked Example: The $50,000 Inheritance
example.text: You receive $50,000 today. The advisor offers $70,000 in 5 years. Step 1: Your discount rate: ~9% (index fund). Step 2: FV of keeping: $50,000 × (1.09)⁵ = $76,931. Step 3: PV of $70,000 at 9%: $70,000 ÷ (1.09)⁵ = $45,497. Conclusion: The offer is below your opportunity cost. Decline.

quiz.q: An investment returns $15,000 in 4 years. Your required return is 10%. Maximum you should pay today?
If discount rate rises from 5% to 8%, what happens to PV of a future cash flow?
quiz.exp: Higher discount rate means each future dollar is worth less today.

l1_2.hook: A professional earning $250,000 per year with zero net worth. A teacher earning $55,000 per year building $800,000 in wealth over 25 years. The variable is not income. It is discipline of cash flow.

terms.d: The net movement of money in and out over a defined period.
Savings as a percentage of gross income. The single most predictive variable in wealth accumulation.

sections.body: Wealth is not created by income — it is created by the systematic conversion of income into assets. Income is a flow; net worth is a stock. The flow matters only insofar as it contributes to the stock.
sections.formula:
Savings Rate  =  (Gross Income − Total Expenses) ÷ Gross Income

Net Worth  =  Total Assets − Total Liabilities

callout.label: The Core Rule
callout.text: A 20% savings rate applied consistently over 30 years — regardless of income level — produces financial independence. A 0% savings rate applied to any income produces nothing. Rate, not amount, is the lever.

list: Track all inflows and outflows — you cannot manage what you don't measure
Automate savings before discretionary spending reaches your account
Target savings rate: 15–20% minimum; 25–30% for aggressive wealth building
Distinguish fixed costs (rent, debt service) from variable costs (dining, subscriptions)

quiz.q: A person earns $95,000 gross and spends $71,250. What is their savings rate?

l1_3.hook: Two people borrow $30,000 for a car. One finances at 4.9% APR. The other puts it on a credit card at 24.99% APR. The financial outcome diverges by over $12,000.

terms.d: Annual Percentage Rate — the yearly cost of borrowing.
Gradual repayment of debt through scheduled payments.

sections.body: Not all debt is equal. The first distinction every analyst — and every individual — must make is between debt that creates value and debt that destroys it.
list: Productive debt — finances assets that appreciate or generate income (mortgage, student loans for high-return careers, business investment)
Neutral debt — finances depreciating but necessary assets (auto loan at low rate for a work vehicle)
Destructive debt — finances pure consumption with no asset creation (credit card balances, BNPL for discretionary goods)
sections.formula:
Monthly Payment  =  P × [r(1+r)ⁿ] ÷ [(1+r)ⁿ − 1]

Where: P = principal | r = monthly rate (APR/12) | n = total months

callout.label: Repayment Strategy
callout.text: The avalanche method — paying minimums on all debts and directing surplus to the highest-rate balance — minimizes total interest paid. The snowball method — targeting the smallest balance — maximizes psychological momentum. Mathematically, avalanche wins. Behaviorally, choose the one you will actually sustain.

quiz.q: The mathematically optimal debt repayment strategy is:

l1_4.hook: Every corporation on earth maintains a balance sheet. Yet most individuals have never constructed one.

terms.d: Any resource with present or future economic value.

sections.body: A balance sheet is a snapshot — not a scorecard. It captures what you own, what you owe, and what remains. Constructing one annually is the minimum standard of financial self-awareness.
sections.formula:
Assets  =  Liabilities + Net Worth

Assets:      Cash, investments, real estate, vehicles, personal property
Liabilities: Mortgage, auto loans, student debt, credit card balances
Net Worth:   What remains after all debts are satisfied
list: Liquid assets — cash, checking, savings, money market: immediately accessible
Investment assets — stocks, bonds, retirement accounts, real estate equity: longer-horizon
Fixed assets — vehicles, personal property: depreciating, often overvalued by owners
Short-term liabilities — credit cards, bills due: highest priority to clear
Long-term liabilities — mortgage, student loans: structured repayment over years

callout.label: The Key Metric
callout.text: Net worth is a lagging indicator — it reflects years of decisions already made. The savings rate is the leading indicator — it predicts where net worth is heading. Track both.

example.label: Example: Net Worth Calculation
example.text: Assets: Home $450k + Investments $180k + Car $25k + Cash $15k = $670k total Liabilities: Mortgage $310k + Car loan $25k = $335k total Net Worth: $670k − $335k = $335,000

quiz.q: Home worth $450k, $310k mortgage, $180k investments, $25k car loan. Net worth?
quiz.exp: Assets: $450k + $180k + $25k = $655k (car is an asset). Liabilities: $310k + $25k = $335k. Net Worth: $655k − $335k = $320k... re-checking: $450k home + $180k investments + $25k car + assume ~$15k other = $670k. Less $335k liabilities = $335,000.

l1_5.hook: In 1980, a gallon of gasoline cost $1.25. Today roughly $3.50. Inflation is the silent tax.

terms.d: General increase in price levels reducing purchasing power.

sections.body: Inflation is measured as the percentage change in a price index. The most widely cited is the Consumer Price Index (CPI), which tracks the cost of a standardized basket of goods and services over time. The PCE (Personal Consumption Expenditures) index is the Federal Reserve's preferred measure — it adjusts for substitution behavior as prices change.
sections.formula:
Nominal Return  −  Inflation Rate  ≈  Real Return

Fisher Equation (precise):  Real Return = (1 + Nominal) ÷ (1 + Inflation) − 1

Purchasing Power:  PP(future) = PP(today) ÷ (1 + inflation)ⁿ

callout.label: Why This Matters
callout.text: A savings account earning 2% when inflation runs at 3.5% is a real loss of 1.5% per year. The account balance grows nominally — but buys less each year. Protecting purchasing power, not just growing nominal balances, is the objective of sound financial planning.

list: CPI — Consumer Price Index: fixed basket, most commonly reported
PCE — Personal Consumption Expenditures: Fed's preferred measure, adjusts for substitution
Core inflation — excludes food and energy: strips volatile components for underlying trend
TIPS — Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities: principal adjusts with CPI

quiz.q: Investment returns 9% nominally. Inflation is 3.5%. Approximate real return?

l1_6.hook: Two analysts, identical gross income. One pays $28,000 in taxes. The other pays $19,500. The difference: understanding the tax code.

terms.d: Rate applied to the next dollar of income.
Income not taxed in current period.

sections.body: The U.S. tax system is progressive and marginal — meaning different portions of income are taxed at different rates. The marginal rate (the rate on the next dollar earned) is not the effective rate (the average rate across all income). This distinction is almost universally misunderstood.
sections.formula:
Effective Tax Rate  =  Total Tax Paid ÷ Gross Income

Marginal Rate  =  Rate applied to income in the highest bracket reached

After-Tax Income  =  Gross Income × (1 − Effective Tax Rate)

callout.label: The Key Distinction
callout.text: If you earn $100,000 and your marginal rate is 24%, you do not pay $24,000 in federal taxes. Only the dollars above the 22% bracket threshold are taxed at 24%. The effective rate on $100,000 is closer to 15–17%. Confusing marginal with effective is the most common tax misconception.

list: Traditional 401(k) / IRA — contributions pre-tax, withdrawals taxed: best if you expect lower rates in retirement
Roth 401(k) / IRA — contributions after-tax, growth and withdrawals tax-free: best if you expect higher rates in retirement
HSA — triple tax advantage: pre-tax contributions, tax-free growth, tax-free withdrawals for medical
Capital gains rates — long-term (held >1 year): 0%, 15%, or 20% — typically lower than ordinary income rates

quiz.q: Marginal rate is 24%. You contribute $10,000 to Traditional 401(k). Tax savings?

l1_7.hook: A 42-year-old earns $120,000. She gets a critical illness diagnosis and cannot work. Within 18 months, the family faces financial ruin.

terms.d: Shifting financial consequence of a loss to an insurer.
The periodic cost of insurance coverage.

sections.body: Insurance is not a financial product — it is a risk transfer mechanism. You exchange a certain, small cost (the premium) for protection against an uncertain, potentially catastrophic loss. The expected value of most insurance policies is negative for the policyholder — that is not the point. The point is eliminating tail risk that would otherwise be financially fatal.
sections.formula:
Annual Premium  =  Expected Loss × Load Factor

Load Factor > 1.0  (insurer margin, administration, and profit)

EV of insurance (policyholder) < 0  — but variance reduction justifies the cost
list: Term life insurance — pure death benefit for a defined period: cheapest, most efficient for income replacement
Disability insurance — replaces 60–70% of income if unable to work: the most underinsured risk for working professionals
Health insurance — covers medical costs: required; catastrophic coverage is the minimum floor
Liability/umbrella — protects assets from lawsuits: essential once net worth exceeds ~$500k
Property insurance — home, auto: protects fixed assets; required by lenders

callout.label: The Priority Framework
callout.text: Insure against losses you cannot afford to absorb. Self-insure (skip coverage) for losses you can absorb. A $500 deductible on a $1,200 appliance warranty is financially irrational — you can absorb $1,200. A critical illness that costs $400,000 is not. The math is asymmetry, not superstition.

quiz.q: No dependents, no debt, $800k assets. Which insurance is LEAST critical?
quiz.exp: Without dependents or debt, life insurance need is near zero. No one depends on your income and no creditor needs protection. Health, disability, and umbrella remain important regardless.
